titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: A ladies' fair is to be held at the Musical Hall, on Bush street, on Tuesday evening next, the proceeds of which are to be devoted to the erection of St. Mary's Church. A great variety of curiosities will be on exhibition, some of which, from their rarity and peculiarity, will be well worth the attention of the scientific and the curious. Some of the most singular objects will be disposed of by lot or raffle, or as may at the time be thought proper and advisable. One of the most singular things on exhibition will be the dress of an old Indian woman, who for seventeen years lived

l.span: Alone in the wide, wide sea;
And ne'er a soul took pity on

div.p: This woman, it appears, was accidentally left on the Island of San Nicholas—which is about sixty miles from Santa Barbara—many years ago, when the rest of the few who lived there were taken off to the main land. It was known that she had been left, and attempts were made to find her, without success. Traces of her existence there have been seen from time to time, but not till recently has she ever been seen by any one, when a Mr. Niedever, of Santa Barbara, who was coasting about San Nicholas in search of otters, suddenly came upon her. She manifested little surprise at Mr. Niedever's appearance, and willingly left her solitary abode. The dress of bird skins which she wore is a great curiosity. How she killed the birds is unknown, or how she contrived to form the needle with which she sewed the skins together. A bottle made of wicker work was also brought away, and it passes belief that human skill could, with no tools but a small piece of rusty hoop iron, make anything so perfect. What long and dreary desolation this woman endured, or whether her life was ever diversified by anything to break the monotony of her loneliness, is unknown. She understood not a word of any language spoken by the nearest Indians, and died before anything could be learned of the incidents of her dark existence.

On the 18th ult. the old woman died of a severe dysentery, brought on it is supposed by eating immoderately of fruit and water melons. The change in her habits and mode of living, may also probably have contributed to hasten her death. Before her death Mrs. George Niedever, the wife of the gentleman who brought her off from Santa [sic] Nicholas, taught her the Lord's Prayer, the Hail Mary and the Creed; and as she expressed a desire to bo [be] baptized, Father Sanchez, one of the pious Franciscan fathers attached to the Mission of Santa Barbara, administered to her the sacrament of baptism and admitted her into the Church.
